statusLine: Americas oil markets face elevated prices near $94 WTI despite 10% weekly decline, as Middle East shipping disruptions strain global supply chains while US crude inventories draw sharply.
fullAnalysis:
US oil markets remain under significant stress despite WTI crude falling 10.24% in the week to April 17th to $93.84/bbl, still elevated well above typical trading ranges. The $8.04 discount to Brent crude reflects ongoing supply chain disruptions that favor Atlantic Basin crudes over Americas benchmarks. US production held steady at 13.585 million bpd, maintaining America's position as the world's top producer, but this output level appears insufficient to offset broader global supply concerns.

Commercial crude inventories declined by 4.1 million barrels to 405 million barrels, indicating robust domestic demand despite high prices. More concerning, gasoline stocks fell 4.6 million barrels and distillates dropped 3.4 million barrels, suggesting the refining system is struggling to meet seasonal demand ahead of summer driving season. The Strategic Petroleum Reserve remains at critically low levels of 80 million barrels, providing minimal buffer capacity.

Critical maritime advisories highlight severe disruption risks across key global chokepoints, particularly Houthi attacks in the Red Sea/Bab el Mandeb and Iranian threats in the Strait of Hormuz. These disruptions are forcing longer tanker routes and higher freight costs, contributing to the elevated WTI-Brent spread. The Panama Canal remains operationally stable, preserving the critical link between US Gulf Coast refiners and West Coast markets.

Retail fuel prices remain painfully high for consumers, with gasoline at $4.18/gallon and diesel at $5.40/gallon, though both declined modestly from prior weeks. These price levels will continue pressuring household budgets and potentially dampening economic activity across the hemisphere. Canada's oil sands production likely faces continued pipeline bottlenecks, while Guyana's rapidly expanding Stabroek production provides one of the few bright spots for regional supply growth.
keyPoints: WTI crude fell 10.24% in week to April 17th but remains elevated at $93.84/bbl amid global supply concerns
US crude inventories declined 4.1 million barrels with gasoline and distillate stocks also drawing sharply
Critical maritime advisories warn of Houthi and Iranian attacks disrupting key shipping lanes
WTI trading $8.04 below Brent reflects supply chain disruptions favoring Atlantic Basin crudes
US production steady at 13.585 million bpd but insufficient to offset global supply chain stress
Retail gasoline at $4.18/gallon continues pressuring consumers despite modest weekly decline
